evidence.title: Management of Acute Pain from Non–Low Back Musculoskeletal Injuries: Guidelines from AAFP and ACP | AAFP
Get NHS help for back or joint problems from community musculoskeletal (MSK) services

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of dull aching pain localized to the left side of the lower rib cage. The pain improves with better posture and is not associated with any recent trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, respiratory symptoms, or neurological deficits. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief.

O: Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain likely related to postural strain or soft tissue injury.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or myofascial pain
2. Costochondritis or rib dysfunction
3. Early intercostal nerve irritation
4. Less likely visceral causes such as pleuritis or referred pain from abdominal organs

P: First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg PO twice daily for 7 days with food
Alternatives: Diclofenac 50 mg PO three times daily for 7 days; Meloxicam 15 mg PO once daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
Medication safety considerations: Use with caution in patients with gastrointestinal risk factors; take with food to minimize GI irritation; avoid in patients with known NSAID allergy or bleeding disorders
Return precautions: Return if pain worsens, new neurological symptoms develop (numbness, weakness), fever or systemic symptoms occur, or if no improvement after 7 days of treatment
Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flag symptoms and normal systemic review
